name: P-Pulmonale
identifyBy: P wave amplitude ≥2.5mm in lead II (tall, peaked, narrow)
P wave duration normal (<120ms) - narrow and pointed
P wave axis: normal to rightward (positive in inferior leads)
Most prominent in II, III, aVF (inferior leads)
May see right axis deviation and right ventricular strain pattern if severe cor pulmonale

measurements.stSegment: Normal (or RV strain in severe cases)

management: P-pulmonale is a marker - treat the underlying cause
Common causes: COPD (most common in adults), pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary fibrosis, severe obesity, PE
Acute PE: peaked P waves may develop acutely with sinus tachycardia + S1Q3T3
COPD management: bronchodilators, smoking cessation, oxygen (target SpO2 88–92% in hypercapnic), CPAP/BIPAP
Pulmonary hypertension: specialist management (prostacyclins, endothelin antagonists, PDE5 inhibitors)
Echocardiogram: confirms right heart enlargement and estimates PA pressure
pearls: P-pulmonale = right atrial enlargement (peaked P) vs P-mitrale = left atrial enlargement (broad bifid P)
Acute onset of P-pulmonale pattern: think PE or acute severe asthma - right heart pressure acutely elevated
In COPD: P-pulmonale may be present chronically with right axis deviation and low voltage QRS (air trapping)
S1Q3T3 pattern in PE: S wave in I, Q wave and T inversion in III - combined with peaked P and sinus tachycardia
differentials: P-mitrale (broad, bifid P wave - left atrial enlargement)
Normal high P wave variant (measure amplitude carefully - must be ≥2.5mm)
Hyperkalaemia (peaked P early, then P wave disappears)
Biatrial enlargement (both peaked and bifid P wave components)

qaNotes: Structural/atrial enlargement pattern preserves P/QRS/ST-T morphology from source content.
